one_line_takeaway: A cascaded AST-then-semantic two-stage detector (SIFT-VulMiner + SAFE-VulMiner with risk-biased attention) paired with a template-constrained, iteratively-refined LLM localizer (ISAL) and a new 26.6k-file PHP vulnerability dataset (PHPVD) together push PHP vulnerability detection to up to 99.7% accuracy and 80.95% localization rate, and the deployed pipeline surfaced 429 real, previously-unknown vulnerabilities (351 with assigned CVEs).
key_finding: On PVts, the best Stage-I-only configuration (CodeT5 encoder + GRU classifier) reaches 83.5% accuracy with FNR of only 1.3% (vs. FNR of 34.3% for HiddenCPG and 14.0% for RecurScan cited as baselines), showing the AST/flow-based recall-oriented filter successfully catches most true positives before Stage II runs. Adding Stage II's data preprocessing and risk-biased attention (Table 2) lifts accuracy substantially across all encoder/classifier pairs, with the top full-pipeline configuration (CodeBERT + Transformer) reaching 99.7% accuracy (a +13.3-point gain over the pre-adjustment baseline for that pairing) - this is the number the abstract cites as the headline '99.7% detection accuracy, 99.5% F1.' On the larger, more realistic PHPVD dataset, overall Stage-I accuracy is lower (89.4%) and FPR is halved from 23.5% to 12.9% via the cascaded two-stage design. Removing the risk-biased attention causes accuracy drops of up to 23.3 points (CodeBERT+Transformer variant), and removing AST linearization raises Stage-I FNR from 1.3% to 7.8% (Table 3). For localization, ISAL reaches an overall 80.95% localization rate vs. 65.32% for the prompt-only zero-knowledge LLM baseline (Table 4), with per-type rates of 84.97% (Injection), 48.26% (URF), and 94.94% (XSS). ISAL's own ablation (Table 5) shows the constraint set contributes the largest single drop when removed (80.95% -> 69.34%), followed by the template library (-> 71.05%) and iterative refinement (-> 77.12%); localization rate saturates after two refinement iterations. Deployed on real-world open-source PHP repositories, the pipeline found 429 previously unknown vulnerabilities, of which 351 were confirmed and assigned CVE identifiers via responsible disclosure.
core_contribution: AutoVulnPHP, an end-to-end PHP vulnerability detection and localization framework with three novel components: (1) SIFT-VulMiner, a Stage-I high-recall detector that builds a flow-enhanced AST (control-flow and data-flow edges added to a PHP-Parser AST), linearizes it via depth-first traversal, encodes it with CodeT5, and classifies with a recall-tuned GRU; (2) SAFE-VulMiner, a Stage-II high-precision verifier that re-tokenizes Stage-I candidates, applies a Risk-Biased Self-Attention mechanism (a learned bias matrix B added to standard scaled dot-product attention to up-weight sensitive tokens like eval, query, $_GET) on top of a CodeBERT-style Transformer, and fuses scores with Stage I via score_final = lambda*scoreI + (1-lambda)*scoreII; (3) ISAL (Incremental Sequence Analysis for Localization), which formulates automated vulnerability localization as constrained optimization over a candidate space generated by parameterized micro-templates completed by an LLM (Deepseek-R1) under hard security/functional constraints extracted from the code's IR, with dual scoring (security score via re-running the frozen detectors + semantic-consistency score) and iterative refinement driven by compile/unit-test failure feedback. The paper also contributes PHPVD, a new PHP vulnerability dataset of 26,614 files (5.2M LOC) spanning seven vulnerability types (IDOR, Injection, SDE, SM, URF, XSS, File Inclusion), built from 2020-2025 PHP CVEs (NVD) matched to GitHub/Sourcecodester/Kashipara/CampCodes projects plus OWASP examples, with a three-part data augmentation pipeline (code refactoring, variable renaming, syntax-tree transformation) to counter the <1% vulnerable-sample class imbalance.
threat_model: The paper targets defenders (security researchers/maintainers) trying to find and localize known-vulnerability-class flaws (SQL injection, XSS, command injection, file inclusion, IDOR, CSRF/path-traversal-style bugs, sensitive data exposure, security misconfiguration, unvalidated redirects) in PHP source code before or after deployment. It is a static/ML-based code-auditing tool, not an attack paper: there is no adversary model against the detector itself (e.g., no evasion/adversarial-example analysis of SIFT-VulMiner/SAFE-VulMiner), and the localization module's 'constraints' are functional/safety correctness constraints on generated patches, not a threat model against the LLM. The paper explicitly discusses controlled-access release of code/dataset and a responsible-disclosure process for the 429 vulnerabilities it found in the wild.
limitations: The paper's own failure analysis (Section 4.2) identifies three causes for the ~19% of localization failures: (1) complex inter-procedural context spanning multiple files/classes that ISAL's slice-level view cannot see; (2) constraint conflicts in ~7% of failed cases (e.g., a needed sanitization step conflicts with a legitimate raw-HTML formatting requirement), causing the verifier to reject an otherwise-generated fix to preserve functionality; (3) LLM hallucination on highly dynamic PHP constructs (variable variables, call_user_func), producing patches that are semantically secure but syntactically invalid for the target PHP version. Additional limitations evident from the text: detection accuracy is markedly lower on the more realistic/larger PHPVD dataset (89.4%) than on the older public PVts benchmark and the after-adjustment numbers (up to 99.7%), so the headline 99.7%/99.5% figures reflect the best single encoder/classifier pairing after Stage-II adjustments rather than the framework's typical or worst-case performance. The paper does not report an evaluation of SIFT-VulMiner/SAFE-VulMiner robustness to adversarial or evasive code (obfuscation beyond the augmentation transforms used for training). The dataset labeling relies on CVE-to-file association from NVD/GitHub matching, which can introduce label noise not directly quantified. Full code/dataset release is gated behind an authorization request process rather than being fully open, limiting independent reproducibility. The future-work section itself flags PHP-only scope, lack of stronger verification, and no developer-feedback loop as open gaps.

datasets_used: PHPVD (paper's own contribution): 26,614 PHP files, 5.2M LOC, seven vulnerability types (IDOR, Injection, SDE, SM, URF, XSS, File Inclusion), built from NVD PHP CVEs 2020-2025 matched to GitHub/Sourcecodester/Kashipara/CampCodes repos plus OWASP examples
PVts - PHP-Vulnerability-test-suite (public benchmark, Walden/Stivalet et al., used as primary benchmark in Table 1/Table 2)
quantitative_results: Stage I (SIFT-VulMiner, CodeT5+GRU) on PVts: 83.5% accuracy, 65.9% precision, 79.0% F1, 23.5% FPR, 1.3% FNR (Table 1)
Full pipeline best config (CodeBERT+Transformer) after preprocessing + risk-biased attention: 99.7% accuracy (+13.3 points) (Table 2) - source of abstract's headline 99.7%/99.5% figures
PHPVD overall accuracy: 89.4%; FPR reduced from 23.5% to 12.9% via two-stage cascade
AST ablation (Table 3): full SIFT-VulMiner (AST+Enc+GRU) 83.5% ACC/23.5% FPR/1.3% FNR vs. w/o AST (raw code only) 79.2% ACC/28.9% FPR/7.8% FNR
Risk-biased attention ablation: removing it drops accuracy up to 23.3 points (CodeBERT+Transformer)
ISAL overall localization rate: 80.95% vs. zero-knowledge LLM baseline 65.32% (Table 4)
ISAL localization rate by type: Injection 84.97%, URF 48.26%, XSS 94.94%, Total 80.95% (Table 4)
ISAL ablation (Table 5): Full ISAL 80.95%; w/o Iterative Refinement 77.12%; w/o Constraint Set 69.34%; w/o Template Library 71.05%; Prompt-only LLM 65.32%
Real-world deployment: 429 previously unknown vulnerabilities discovered; 351 assigned CVE identifiers
baselines_compared: HiddenCPG (CPG subgraph-isomorphism vulnerable clone detection) - FNR 34.3% vs. AutoVulnPHP's 1.3%
RecurScan (recurring-vulnerability detection via symbolic patch comparison) - FNR 14.0% vs. AutoVulnPHP's 1.3%
Walden J. (text-mining vs. software-metrics vulnerability prediction, 223-vulnerability public dataset)
Abunadi I. (cross-project ML vulnerability prediction, five classifiers)
zero-knowledge LLM (prompt-only localization, no templates or constraints) - 65.32% overall localization rate vs. ISAL's 80.95%
